event_and_applicant_information.tip_challenge_event_location: Mustang TIP Challenge – Fort Worth, TX (Will Rogers Memorial Center)

trainer_evaluation.how_long_have_you_known_this_trainer:
I have known Sarah Elizabeth McAllister for a little over six years, since the spring of 2019. We first met when she moved her two personal geldings into my boarding and training facility, Cartwright Performance & Horsemanship, located just outside Decatur, Texas. At that time, she was primarily riding well-started ranch horses but was very interested in learning more about colt-starting and the gentling of mustangs.

Over the first year, I saw her several times each week as a boarder and lesson client. She was very consistent about taking weekly lessons with me, attending every clinic we hosted on groundwork and young horse development, and volunteering to help with chores and handling the quieter lesson horses. Around late 2020, after she had spent quite a bit of time auditing and helping at our colt-starting clinics, she began taking in a couple of outside horses for basic groundwork and tune‑ups under my supervision.

By early 2021, when she adopted her first BLM mustang through a TIP-related program, I was closely involved, and we worked side by side almost daily for the first sixty days. Since then, our relationship has evolved into a mix of colleague, mentor, and friend. She continues to keep one horse at my facility on a semi-regular basis (for access to our arena and obstacles), and I also haul to her place a few times a month to work with a small group of riders she has begun mentoring.

In addition to the professional context, I know her personally: I’ve been to her home on multiple occasions, attended a small barn-warming barbecue when she finished her perimeter fencing, and watched her compete in several local and regional events. I also know her husband, Mark, and have met her parents and younger sister at shows. So my knowledge of Sarah is both as a horsewoman and as a person in her day‑to‑day life over a substantial period of time.
trainer_evaluation.how_often_do_you_see_this_trainer_ride_o:
Currently, I see Sarah ride or work horses in person an average of two to three times per week, though this varies slightly by season.

During the spring and summer, when she typically has one or two mustangs in training along with a couple of client horses, I see her at least three times weekly. On Mondays and Wednesdays, she usually hauls into my facility in the late afternoon. I routinely observe her doing groundwork in the round pen, then transitioning to the main arena for under-saddle work, especially with horses that are in the early riding stages. On at least one weekend day per week, we are either at a local jackpot, trail clinic, or open arena together, which gives me another several hours of observation.

In the fall and winter, when daylight is shorter and the show schedule is lighter, I still see her in person at least once or twice a week. She also sends me regular video updates of her rides and groundwork sessions, especially when she’s working through something challenging with a green or sensitive horse. I’d estimate I watch an additional two to three recorded sessions each week, which allows me to see her consistency and progression with each horse over time.

To give a concrete example, with her most recent TIP mustang gelding, “Ranger,” I watched her work him in person three or four days a week for the first three weeks—primarily groundwork, desensitizing, and basic leading. After that, I observed her rides on him roughly twice a week in my arena, and she shared short video clips from the other days. Over the ninety-day period, there were very few days when I did not have at least some visibility into what she was doing with that horse.

So while I may not be physically present for every single session, I have a very clear, ongoing picture of how often and how consistently Sarah is riding and working horses—she is one of the more diligent and hands‑on trainers I know in terms of the number of quality hours she puts in each week.
trainer_evaluation.do_you_feel_that_their_facilities_are_su:
yes. I have personally been to Sarah’s property multiple times and have seen the progression of her facilities over the last few years, specifically with the goal of making them appropriate for mustangs and other unhandled horses.

Her place is a small but very functional five‑acre setup just outside Springtown, Texas. The key elements that I believe make it suitable for working with a wild horse are:

1. **Secure Perimeter and Containment:**
   - The entire property is enclosed with no‑climb horse fencing topped with a single strand of hot wire, which is important for preventing accidental escapes if a mustang were to get loose.
   - Within that perimeter, she has a dedicated 60' x 60' heavy‑duty pipe panel pen with welded connections and no sharp edges. The panels are at least 6 feet high, which is appropriate for a fresh, untouched mustang that may test boundaries.

2. **Safe Handling and Chute Setup:**
   - She has installed a smaller, roughly 16' x 16' pen attached to the main pen that serves as a catch pen or handling area. The gate between the two is solid and swings smoothly, allowing her to move a horse into a smaller area for closer work without creating tight, dangerous corners.
   - She also has a chute‑style alleyway made from solid‑sided panels where a mustang can be safely contained for farrier work, administering vaccinations, or vet checks. The sides are high enough to discourage jumping, and the footing is level and not slick.

3. **Footing and Environment:**
   - The primary pens have well‑drained, packed dirt footing with some sand mixed in, which provides decent traction without being too deep or unstable for a horse that may be moving quickly or unpredictably.
   - There is good visibility and no clutter—no loose wire, broken boards, or random equipment lying around that could tangle a panicked horse.

4. **Shelter, Water, and General Care:**
   - Each working and holding area has access to clean, fresh water at all times via automatic waterers that she checks daily.
   - She has run‑in sheds that provide shade and protection from the wind and rain, and I’ve seen mustangs she’s worked with use these shelters comfortably once they relax.

5. **Supportive Infrastructure and Supervision:**
   - Sarah’s house is within clear sight and earshot of the mustang pen, so if there is any commotion at night, she is aware of it quickly.
   - She has good lighting around the pens, which is important for safety during late‑evening checks or if an emergency arises.

In addition to the physical setup, what reassures me most is how she *uses* the facilities. She is very careful about introducing new mustangs to the space, starting them in the most secure, smallest area and gradually increasing their freedom as they become more manageable. She does not put wild horses directly into large open pastures, and she is careful about not turning them out with other horses until they are halter‑broke and reliably catchable.

From everything I’ve seen, her facilities not only meet but exceed what I would consider the minimum safe standard for working with a wild or unhandled horse, and she continually looks for ways to make them even more secure and horse‑friendly.
trainer_evaluation.do_you_have_any_concerns_about_the_horse:
no. I do not have concerns about the welfare, safety, or general treatment of the horses in Sarah’s care. On the contrary, she is often one of the people I point to as an example of how to balance firmness and clarity with a very high standard of kindness and patience.

To elaborate with specifics:

1. **Daily Care and Condition:**
   - Every horse I have seen at her place has been at an appropriate body condition—neither underweight nor obese. Her mustangs, in particular, come in a bit lean (as is typical) and gradually bloom over the first sixty to ninety days. Coats are shiny, hooves are in good shape, and they generally present as relaxed and curious rather than shut down or frantic.

2. **Feeding and Watering Practices:**
   - Sarah is meticulous about consistent hay and clean water. She uses slow‑feed nets in some cases to avoid gorging and to keep forage available longer, which I think is especially important for previously feral horses learning to trust domestic routines.
   - She adjusts grain and supplements conservatively, always consulting with a vet or experienced nutritionist before making big changes.

3. **Handling and Training Methods:**
   - Her training approach is very much pressure‑and‑release based, with a strong emphasis on reading the horse’s mental state. I have never seen her lose her temper, strike a horse out of frustration, or push one to the point of meltdown.
   - When a horse shows signs of being mentally overwhelmed—tight eyes, raised head, frantic feet—she is quick to back off, give them a break, and then return to the task in smaller, more manageable steps.

4. **Veterinary and Farrier Care:**
   - Sarah uses a reputable equine veterinarian from a local practice (I know them personally) and does not hesitate to call for help. I recall one specific instance when a mustang came up slightly off in the left front. Instead of “waiting to see,” she had the vet out within 24 hours, followed all instructions, and limited the horse’s work until it was cleared.
   - Her farrier schedule is consistent, and she makes a point of preparing mustangs for the farrier—working on picking up feet, standing quietly, and accepting the tools—well before the actual appointment.

5. **Workload and Rest:**
   - She is very aware of not overworking a young or green horse. Her typical session with a fresh mustang might only be 20–30 minutes of focused work, with plenty of breaks. As they progress, session length increases, but she rotates tasks (groundwork, light riding, hand‑grazing, desensitizing) to keep them mentally fresh.

If I had any concern at all, it would be that she sometimes second‑guesses herself and is almost *too* cautious about progressing, especially if a horse has had a difficult past. However, I see that as a minor thing and more of a personality trait than a welfare issue. Overall, I have zero concerns that any horse in her care would be neglected, mishandled, or put at unnecessary risk. If that were ever to change, I am close enough to her program that I would notice and speak up, but I do not anticipate that happening given her track record and character.
trainer_evaluation.would_you_recommend_this_trainer_to_peop:
yes. I would absolutely recommend Sarah McAllister to people who have unbroken, green, or behaviorally challenging horses, and I have already done so on multiple occasions.

The main reasons for my strong recommendation are:

1. **Proven Experience with Green and Wild Horses:**
   - Over the past several years, I’ve watched Sarah successfully start multiple horses under saddle, including BLM mustangs and a couple of domestic horses that had been mishandled previously. In each case, she approached the horse as an individual, adjusted her timeline based on what the horse could mentally handle, and ended up with an animal that was not only rideable but also more relaxed and confident.

2. **Calm, Consistent Demeanor:**
   - Sarah’s temperament is exceptionally well‑suited for difficult horses. She is soft‑spoken, patient, and not easily rattled. I have seen her remain steady and composed while a young gelding was bucking and striking at the panel, and instead of reacting with force, she simply adjusted her approach, gave him a moment to regroup, and found a way to redirect that energy productively.

3. **Willingness to Educate Owners:**
   - She does not just “fix” horses and send them home. She insists that owners come for follow‑up lessons, watch sessions, and learn the groundwork and riding cues she has installed. This is critical for long‑term success, especially with difficult horses, and she is very good at breaking things down for less experienced owners.

4. **Ethical Limits and Honesty:**
   - Importantly, Sarah is not afraid to say “this is beyond what I can safely do” or “this horse needs a different environment or a more specialized trainer.” I have seen her turn down a horse that had a serious history of flipping over under saddle because she felt that, given her current facility setup and her own physical limitations (she has a mild, old back injury), it would not be fair to the horse or to herself to take that on. That honesty is exactly what I want to see in someone dealing with difficult horses.

5. **Concrete Success Stories:**
   - One example is a bay mare named “Maggie” who had become extremely reactive about being saddled after a bad experience. The owner was at their wits’ end and considering selling her. Over the course of about three months, Sarah very methodically rebuilt Maggie’s trust around touch, the pad, and eventually the saddle, using lots of short, positive sessions. By the end, Maggie was quietly accepting the saddle, standing to be mounted, and going walk‑trot under a calm, balanced seat. The owner still sends updates and rides her regularly.

Because of all this, I feel comfortable recommending Sarah to people with unbroken or difficult horses. I do, however, always tell owners that they must be willing to participate in the process and continue the work at home—something Sarah also emphasizes strongly.
trainer_evaluation.personal_comments_regarding_skill_work_e:
Sarah is one of the most thoughtful and conscientious horsewomen I have worked with in my thirty‑plus years in the industry. Her skills are not flashy in the sense of big, dramatic “before and after” moments; instead, she is consistent, detail‑oriented, and deeply committed to doing right by each individual horse.

**Skill Level and Training Approach:**
Sarah has a very solid foundation in groundwork, body control, and reading subtle changes in a horse’s expression and posture. She is not afraid to ask for help or feedback, which I think actually elevates her skill level, because she is constantly learning. She keeps a training notebook for each horse, where she writes down what they worked on, how the horse responded, and any small changes she wants to make the next day. That level of tracking is not something I see often, even among professionals.

Her riding is quiet and balanced, with soft hands and a secure seat. She does an excellent job of staying out of the horse’s way while still providing clear direction. I have seen her ride through some pretty athletic crowhops and spooks without escalating the situation—she just sits deep, breathes, and waits for the right moment to reward.

**Work Ethic:**
Sarah is extraordinarily reliable. If she says she will be at the barn at 7:00 a.m., she is usually there at 6:45 a.m. She keeps her pens clean, her tack in good repair, and her schedule organized. Even on days when the weather is bad or she has had a rough time at her day job (she works part‑time as a veterinary technician), she still shows up for the horses and gives them her full attention. I have never known her to cut corners on care or rush through a session just to check a box.

**Values and Character:**
What stands out to me most about Sarah is her integrity. She does not exaggerate a horse’s progress to impress owners or on social media. If a horse is struggling with something, she is honest about it and frames it as part of the process rather than a failure. She is respectful and kind in how she speaks about horses and people alike.

She also has a strong sense of responsibility toward mustangs in particular. She often says that when you take on a wild horse, you are not just training for yourself—you are shaping that horse’s entire future and its chances of staying in a good home. That mindset shows in the way she prioritizes foundational skills like catching, leading, picking up feet, loading, and accepting basic handling. She is not just training for a competition; she is training for the rest of the horse’s life.

**Personal Details and Interactions:**
On a more personal note, I’ve seen how she balances her horse work with family life. Her husband, Mark, is supportive but not a “horse person,” and she has been very intentional about setting up her routine so that chores and training sessions are done efficiently and safely, leaving time for her marriage and for rest. This tells me she has the maturity and stability to handle the demands of a program like the TIP Challenge.

In short, I believe Sarah has the horsemanship skills, the work ethic, and the core values necessary to not only succeed in the TIP Challenge but also to represent the program in a positive, professional manner. I trust her judgment with horses, and I would not hesitate to send one of my own more challenging horses to her if the need arose.
